Q1: Honestly, my study routine was all over the place. I'd tell myself I was going to study for like 26 hours a week, and sometimes I did, but it wasn't organized at all. I'd listen to lectures while doing other stuff, or I'd cram a bunch of material in one sitting when I remembered I had something due. I learn better by hearing things anyway, so I'd replay audio and videos a lot, but I never really had a set schedule written down or anything.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied based on deadlines and whatever else was going on in my life. Some weeks I'd barely touch anything, then suddenly realize something was due and go hard for a few days straight. With six online courses, it was tough to keep track of everything, so I'd just react to whatever felt most urgent. Regular times weren't really my thing—I'm more of a 'deal with it when it hits' kind of person.
Q3: Usually I'd log in because I got a notification about a deadline or because I remembered I hadn't checked in for a while. Sometimes I'd see other people in my courses posting in discussions and that would remind me to actually open the LMS. The gamified tasks actually got me logging in more because I wanted to see my progress and earn those badges or whatever. It felt less like work when there was that game element to it.
Q4: Yeah, for a while there the gamified stuff made it feel more natural to check in. I'd wake up and want to see if I'd leveled up or completed a challenge, so I'd log in almost like checking social media. It became routine for maybe a few weeks, but then the anxiety about my actual grades started creeping in and that kind of killed the vibe. The habit faded once the stress got real.
Q5: The video lectures were definitely the most useful for me because I'm an auditory learner. I could listen to them while doing other things or replay parts I didn't catch the first time. The discussion boards were also helpful because hearing—or reading—how other students understood stuff helped me process it differently. The gamified tasks made things feel less boring, which kept me coming back even when I didn't feel like studying.
Q6: I pretty much ignored most of the reading materials and PDFs. I just don't learn well from reading walls of text, and with limited resources, I couldn't always print stuff out or access it easily. I'd also skip optional quizzes sometimes because they felt like extra work when I was already overwhelmed. If something didn't have a clear deadline or points attached, it usually got pushed to the bottom of my list.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it either counted toward my grade or if it was actually engaging enough to hold my attention. The gamified tasks were good because they gave me instant feedback and made me feel like I was accomplishing something. If something felt like busy work or if I couldn't see how it connected to what I needed to know for exams, I'd probably skip it or half-ass it.
Q8: Optional activities felt way more skippable, even if they might have helped me. Required stuff had that pressure behind it, so I'd force myself to do it even when I didn't want to. With optional things, my brain would just say 'you don't have to do this' and I'd move on. The gamified optional stuff was different though—it felt more like a choice I wanted to make rather than homework.
Q9: Yeah, definitely. I'd download lecture audio and listen to it on my phone when I wasn't near my computer. Sometimes I'd take notes by hand while listening because that helps me remember stuff better. I also talked through concepts with a couple of friends from class, which helped a lot but obviously wouldn't show up anywhere in the system. A lot of my actual learning happened away from the LMS.
Q10: There were plenty of times I was studying but the LMS wouldn't know. Like, I'd listen to downloaded lectures on repeat while commuting or doing chores. I'd also spend time just thinking through problems in my head or talking them out loud to myself. Sometimes I'd be reviewing my handwritten notes for hours, but since I wasn't clicking anything online, it looked like I wasn't doing anything.
Q11: All the time, yeah. I'd watch a video once on the LMS, download the audio if I could, and then listen to it over and over offline. With my internet situation not always being reliable, it made more sense to grab what I needed and study without being connected. So my one view on the system didn't reflect how many times I actually engaged with that material.
Q12: Midway through the semester things got really hard. My anxiety was through the roof, and I started feeling like no matter how much I did, I was going to fail anyway. That made it harder to even open the LMS because seeing all my courses and deadlines just stressed me out more. When you're already feeling low, even logging in feels like climbing a mountain.
Q13: Absolutely. The workload from six courses was insane, and I was constantly tired and confused about what to prioritize. When I was exhausted, I'd log in but just stare at the screen without really absorbing anything. Competing responsibilities at home also meant I couldn't always focus even when I had time to study. The fatigue made everything feel twice as hard.
Q14: Yeah, there were times I'd have the LMS open and be scrolling through materials, but my brain was somewhere else entirely. I'd watch videos without really listening or click through readings without comprehending anything. It was like going through the motions to feel like I was doing something, but I wasn't actually learning. Those sessions were basically useless even though they'd show up as activity.
Q15: I avoided exam prep a lot because it made my anxiety spike. I knew studying for exams was important—more important than assignments—but the stress of it made me procrastinate hard. I'd do the assignments because they felt more manageable and gave me a sense of accomplishment, but then I'd put off actually preparing for tests until it was too late. That's probably why my exam score was so bad.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me moving sometimes. I'd see a deadline approaching and suddenly kick into high gear, logging in constantly and submitting stuff last minute. Without deadlines, I'd drift and lose track of what needed to be done. The problem is that cramming everything at the end meant I wasn't really learning deeply—just surviving.
Q17: When I got good feedback on assignments, it motivated me to keep going and made me feel like maybe I could actually do this. But when feedback was critical or when I saw my exam scores, it kind of crushed me and made me want to disengage. My mood after those interventions was usually pretty low, which made it harder to bounce back. Feedback was a double-edged sword for me.
Q18: The structure was overwhelming with six courses all happening at once. I never felt like I could fully focus on one thing because there was always something else demanding attention. The gamified elements helped break things into smaller pieces, which made it feel more manageable. But overall, the sheer volume of content and deadlines made it hard to engage deeply with anything.
Q19: The gamification definitely made engagement easier—it gave me small wins and made things feel less like a chore. But the amount of reading-heavy content made it harder since that's not how I learn best. Also, when courses weren't clear about what was most important, I'd waste time on stuff that didn't help my grade. Clearer priorities would have helped a lot.
Q20: I think that's probably students like me who are clicking around, trying to figure things out, maybe stressed and looking for answers but not finding them. Or they're engaging with the gamified stuff and discussions but struggling when it comes to actually producing work. High activity doesn't mean you understand the material—it might just mean you're anxious and checking things constantly.
Q21: That kind of makes sense to me in reverse. Like, some people probably study efficiently offline and don't need to be on the LMS constantly. They read once, get it, and move on. For me, I was the opposite—high activity but still failing exams because being online doesn't mean the information is sticking. Different people learn differently, and the LMS doesn't capture that.
Q22: Course logs can show when you're logging in and what you're clicking on, but they can't show if you're actually understanding anything. They miss all the offline studying, the conversations with classmates, the mental processing that happens when you're not at a computer. They also can't tell if someone is anxious-clicking versus genuinely engaged. The numbers don't tell the whole story.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and feeling connected to the material and the course. It's not just about showing up or clicking things—it's about being mentally present and wanting to understand. For me, I felt engaged when the gamified tasks made things fun, but I felt disengaged when anxiety took over and I was just going through the motions.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much my anxiety affected everything. It wouldn't show that I was listening to lectures on repeat while walking around trying to calm down. It wouldn't capture the conversations I had with friends trying to understand concepts, or how low my mood got after seeing bad grades. My engagement was complicated—sometimes high, sometimes just survival mode—and data can't capture that.
